title: Monaco explosion: The Ukrainian-born oligarch's shady business dealings
subhead: Vadym Yermolaiev, a wealthy businessman, was targeted by a homemade explosive bomb on Monday. Despite renouncing his Ukrainian nationality, he continued business activities connected to his country of origin.

title: France will likely face third heatwave of the year from this weekend
French presidential vote to be held on April 18 and May 2 next year
Macron positions himself as custodian of abolition of capital punishment
subhead: The French president attended the 9th World Congress Against the Death Penalty, as debate on the issue continues to resurface in some parts of French society.

title: Starmer's defense investment plan draws criticism
subhead: As he prepares to hand over power to the UK's likely next leader Andy Burnham, Starmer has asked government departments to forgo 1% of their investment budgets. Even so, he will not meet his pledge to allocate 3% of GDP to defense by 2030.

title: Mbappé fires France into World Cup last 16
subhead: France's victory over Sweden on Tuesday has elevated prolific striker Kylian Mbappé to an incredible World Cup record: 18 in 18 matches – only one behind Argentina's Lionel Messi's all-time record of 19 goals.

title: EU judges' opaque handling of potential conflicts of interest
subhead: Members of the Court of Justice of the European Union have ruled on cases involving companies or economic sectors in which they had interests, according to an investigation conducted since 2024 by Investigate Europe and its partners, including Le Monde.

'Their mouths, eyes, hands, were taped and they were thrown into the hole': Russian soldiers confront Putin with reality of front lines
subhead: A Russian exile media outlet has analyzed nearly 7,000 letters from Russian soldiers and their families to the Kremlin thanks to a security flaw.

title: 250 years of American independence: Sinclair Lewis, the Nobel Prize-winning author who 'burst the smugness' of the average American
subhead: 'America 250' (9/13). The 'Roaring Twenties' saw consumerism, puritanism, and xenophobia rise together in the United States. Sinclair Lewis, a native of Sauk Centre, a rural town in Minnesota, rose to fame with his biting satires of American life, which still resonate today.

title: 'We should not accept that heatwaves cause thousands of deaths. That is not normal'
subhead: In an interview with Le Monde, Julien Dossier, founder of the environmental consulting firm Quattrolibri, offers an initial political assessment of how France responded to the most intense heatwave ever recorded in the country.
